nodes.title: Identify a conic section as the intersection of a plane and a cone
nodes.brief: Recognise that a conic section is the curve produced when a plane cuts a double-napped right circular cone.
nodes.quote: If we take the intersection of a plane with a cone, the section so obtained is called a *conic section*. Thus, conic sections are the curves obtained by intersecting a right circular cone by a plane.
nodes.explanation: Take a double-napped right circular cone: a fixed vertical axis l, a generator line m through the vertex V inclined to the axis at a constant angle alpha, swept around the axis. The vertex V splits the cone into two nappes. When a plane cuts this cone, the curve where they meet is a conic section. So circles, ellipses, parabolas and hyperbolas are not unrelated shapes - they are all slices of one cone, and which one you get depends only on how the cutting plane is tilted relative to the cone's axis.
keyMoves: Picture the double-napped cone generated by rotating a generator about the axis at fixed angle alpha
Define a conic section as the curve where a plane meets the cone
Note vertex, axis, generator and the two nappes
misconceptions: Thinking the four conics are unrelated curves rather than slices of one cone
Forgetting the cone is double-napped (extends both ways from the vertex)

nodes.title: Classify the conic from the angle the cutting plane makes with the axis
nodes.brief: Decide whether the section is a circle, ellipse, parabola or hyperbola by comparing beta (plane-axis angle) with alpha (semi-vertical angle) and 90 degrees.
nodes.quote: When the plane cuts the nappe (other than the vertex) of the cone, we have the following situations:
nodes.explanation: Let alpha be the semi-vertical angle of the cone and beta the angle the cutting plane makes with the axis. When the plane cuts a nappe (not through the vertex): if beta = 90 degrees the section is a circle; if alpha < beta < 90 degrees it is an ellipse; if beta = alpha it is a parabola; and if 0 <= beta < alpha the plane cuts both nappes and the section is a hyperbola. When the plane instead passes through the vertex you get the degenerate cases - a point, a single straight line, or a pair of intersecting straight lines. So the single parameter beta (against alpha and 90) determines the whole family.
keyMoves: Compare beta with 90 degrees and with alpha
beta = 90 -> circle; alpha < beta < 90 -> ellipse; beta = alpha -> parabola; 0 <= beta < alpha -> hyperbola
Recognise vertex-cutting planes give degenerate conics (point, line, pair of lines)
misconceptions: Mixing up which inequality gives an ellipse versus a hyperbola
Forgetting that a hyperbola requires the plane to cut both nappes

nodes.title: Define a circle by its centre and radius
nodes.brief: State that a circle is the locus of points equidistant from a fixed centre, the common distance being the radius.
nodes.quote: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed point in the plane.
nodes.explanation: A circle is the set of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed point. That fixed point is the centre of the circle, and the common distance from the centre to any point on the circle is the radius. This single geometric condition - |CP| = r for every point P on the circle - is the definition from which the equation of the circle is built, and it is what makes the circle a special conic (the section when the cutting plane is perpendicular to the axis).
keyMoves: State the locus condition: every point is at the same distance from the centre
Name the fixed point the centre and the common distance the radius
Write the defining condition |CP| = r
misconceptions: Confusing the radius (centre-to-edge) with the diameter (edge-to-edge)
Thinking the centre lies on the circle

nodes.title: Write the equation of a circle with given centre and radius
nodes.brief: Apply the distance formula to the locus condition to get (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 for centre (h, k) and radius r.
nodes.quote: This is the required equation of the circle with centre at (h,k) and radius r.
nodes.explanation: Let C(h, k) be the centre and r the radius, and P(x, y) any point on the circle. The definition says |CP| = r, so the distance formula gives sqrt((x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2) = r, and squaring yields the standard equation (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2. With centre at the origin this simplifies to x^2 + y^2 = r^2. Given a centre and radius you substitute directly, e.g. centre (-3, 2) and radius 4 give (x + 3)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 16. The equation is just the locus condition with the distance formula put in.
keyMoves: Apply the distance formula to |CP| = r
Square to get (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2
Substitute given centre (h, k) and radius r to write the specific equation
misconceptions: Writing (x + h)^2 + (y + k)^2 instead of subtracting the centre coordinates
Forgetting to square the radius on the right-hand side

nodes.title: Find a circle's centre and radius by completing the square
nodes.brief: Convert a general second-degree circle equation to standard form by completing the square, then read off the centre and radius.
nodes.quote: Now, completing the squares within the parenthesis, we get
nodes.explanation: A circle often appears in expanded form such as x^2 + y^2 + 8x + 10y - 8 = 0. To recover its centre and radius, group the x-terms and y-terms, then complete the square in each: (x^2 + 8x + 16) + (y^2 + 10y + 25) = 8 + 16 + 25, i.e. (x + 4)^2 + (y + 5)^2 = 49. Comparing with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 gives centre (-4, -5) and radius 7. The key is to add the same constants to both sides so the equation stays balanced, and to remember the centre coordinate is the negative of the number inside each bracket.
keyMoves: Group x-terms and y-terms together
Complete the square in x and in y, adding equal amounts to both sides
Compare with the standard form to read off centre (h, k) and radius r
misconceptions: Completing the square on the left without adding the same constants to the right
Reading the centre as (+4, +5) instead of (-4, -5) from (x + 4)^2 + (y + 5)^2

nodes.title: Define a parabola by its focus and directrix
nodes.brief: State that a parabola is the locus of points equidistant from a fixed focus and a fixed directrix line, and name its axis and vertex.
nodes.quote: A parabola is the set of all points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed line and a fixed point (not on the line) in the plane.
nodes.explanation: A parabola is the set of points equidistant from a fixed line and a fixed point not on that line. The fixed line is the directrix and the fixed point F is the focus. The line through the focus perpendicular to the directrix is the axis of the parabola, and the point where the parabola meets its axis is the vertex. (If the fixed point lies on the line you get the degenerate case - a straight line.) This focus-directrix condition PF = PM is the geometric seed for the standard equation.
keyMoves: State the equidistance condition: distance to focus = distance to directrix
Name the fixed line the directrix and the fixed point the focus
Identify the axis (through focus, perpendicular to directrix) and the vertex
misconceptions: Letting the focus lie on the directrix (that gives a degenerate straight line)
Confusing the directrix (a line) with the axis (also a line, but through the focus)

nodes.title: Derive and use the standard equation y^2 = 4ax of a parabola
nodes.brief: Obtain y^2 = 4ax from the focus-directrix condition with vertex at the origin, and identify the four standard orientations.
nodes.quote: These four equations are known as standard equations of parabolas.
nodes.explanation: Place the vertex at the origin with focus (a, 0), a > 0, and directrix x = -a. For any point P(x, y), PF = PB (distance to the directrix) gives sqrt((x - a)^2 + y^2) = sqrt((x + a)^2); squaring and simplifying yields y^2 = 4ax. This is the standard parabola opening right. The four standard forms are y^2 = 4ax (right), y^2 = -4ax (left), x^2 = 4ay (up) and x^2 = -4ay (down). A y^2 term means the axis is along the x-axis; an x^2 term means the axis is along the y-axis. The sign of the coefficient tells which way it opens. To find a parabola from given conditions, match the orientation and solve for a.
keyMoves: Set up PF = PB and square to derive y^2 = 4ax
Read orientation: y^2 term -> axis along x; x^2 term -> axis along y
Use the sign of the coefficient to fix the opening direction, then solve for a
misconceptions: Using y^2 = 4ax for a parabola that actually opens along the y-axis
Getting the opening direction wrong by ignoring the sign of the coefficient

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of a parabola as 4a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of a parabola and show its length is 4a for y^2 = 4ax.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of a parabola is a line segment perpendicular to the axis of the parabola, through the focus and whose end points lie on the parabola
nodes.explanation: The latus rectum is the chord through the focus, perpendicular to the axis, with its ends on the parabola. For y^2 = 4ax, take its end A; by the definition of the parabola AF = AC = FM = 2a, and by symmetry the full chord AB = 2 x 2a = 4a. So the length of the latus rectum is 4a. This lets you read off the focus (a, 0), directrix x = -a and latus rectum 4a directly once you have matched a parabola to its standard form - e.g. y^2 = 8x has 4a = 8, so a = 2, focus (2, 0), directrix x = -2, latus rectum 8.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the axis
Use AF = FM = 2a and symmetry to get total length 4a
Combine with the standard form to report focus, directrix and latus rectum together
misconceptions: Reporting the latus rectum as 2a (half the chord) instead of 4a
Measuring the latus rectum along the axis rather than perpendicular to it

nodes.title: Define an ellipse by the constant sum of focal distances
nodes.brief: State that an ellipse is the locus of points whose distances from two foci add to a constant, and name its centre, major and minor axes and vertices.
nodes.quote: An *ellipse* is the set of all points in a plane, the sum of whose distances from two fixed points in the plane is a constant.
nodes.explanation: An ellipse is the set of points the sum of whose distances from two fixed points (the foci) is a constant; this constant is always greater than the distance between the foci. The midpoint of the segment joining the foci is the centre. The line segment through the foci is the major axis and the perpendicular segment through the centre is the minor axis; the ends of the major axis are the vertices. The sum-of-distances condition PF1 + PF2 = 2a is the geometric basis for the ellipse's equation.
keyMoves: State the locus condition PF1 + PF2 = constant (= 2a)
Name the two fixed points the foci and the midpoint of their segment the centre
Identify major axis, minor axis and the vertices
misconceptions: Using the difference of focal distances (that defines a hyperbola, not an ellipse)
Taking the constant sum to be less than the distance between the foci

nodes.title: Relate the semi-axes and focal distance of an ellipse (a^2 = b^2 + c^2)
nodes.brief: Use the lengths 2a, 2b, 2c (major axis, minor axis, distance between foci) and the relation a^2 = b^2 + c^2.
nodes.quote: We denote the length of the major axis by 2a, the length of the minor axis by 2b and the distance between the foci by 2c.
nodes.explanation: Write the major axis as 2a, the minor axis as 2b and the distance between the foci as 2c, so a, b, c are the semi-major axis, semi-minor axis and focal distance from the centre. Taking the point at the end of the major axis shows the constant sum equals 2a. Taking the point at the end of the minor axis, each focal distance is sqrt(b^2 + c^2), so 2 sqrt(b^2 + c^2) = 2a, giving a = sqrt(b^2 + c^2), i.e. a^2 = b^2 + c^2. Equivalently c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2). This relation is what lets you move between foci, vertices and axis lengths.
keyMoves: Set major axis = 2a, minor axis = 2b, distance between foci = 2c
Show the constant sum is 2a using the major-axis endpoint
Derive a^2 = b^2 + c^2 (so c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2)) using the minor-axis endpoint
misconceptions: Writing c^2 = a^2 + b^2 (the hyperbola relation) instead of a^2 = b^2 + c^2
Confusing the semi-axes a, b with the full axis lengths 2a, 2b

nodes.title: Compute the eccentricity of an ellipse as e = c/a
nodes.brief: Define the eccentricity e = c/a of an ellipse and use it to place the foci at distance ae from the centre.
nodes.quote: The eccentricity of an ellipse is the ratio of the distances from the centre of the ellipse to one of the foci and to one of the vertices of the ellipse
nodes.explanation: The eccentricity e of an ellipse is the ratio of the centre-to-focus distance to the centre-to-vertex distance, i.e. e = c/a. Since 0 < c < a for an ellipse, e lies between 0 and 1; a smaller e means a rounder ellipse, e closer to 1 a more elongated one. In terms of e the focus sits at distance ae from the centre. For example, x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1 has a = 5, b = 3, c = 4, so e = 4/5. The eccentricity is a single number capturing the ellipse's shape.
keyMoves: Compute c from a^2 = b^2 + c^2, then form e = c/a
Check 0 < e < 1 for an ellipse
Locate the foci at distance ae from the centre
misconceptions: Computing e as a/c instead of c/a
Expecting e >= 1 for an ellipse (that holds for a hyperbola)

nodes.title: Use the standard equation x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 of an ellipse
nodes.brief: Apply the standard equation of an ellipse, read which axis is major from the larger denominator, and extract foci, vertices and axis lengths.
nodes.quote: These two equations are known as standard equations of the ellipses.
nodes.explanation: With centre at the origin and foci on the x-axis, the sum-of-distances condition gives x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (a > b). With foci on the y-axis it is x^2/b^2 + y^2/a^2 = 1. The major axis lies along whichever variable has the larger denominator: the foci always lie on the major axis. Comparing a given equation with the standard form gives a and b, then c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2) gives the foci (+-c, 0) or (0, +-c), the vertices (+-a, 0) or (0, +-a), and the axis lengths 2a and 2b. To build an ellipse from conditions, pick the right form by where the foci lie and solve for a^2 and b^2.
keyMoves: Identify the major axis from the larger denominator (foci lie on it)
Read a and b from the standard form and compute c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2)
Report foci, vertices and axis lengths, or solve for a^2, b^2 from given data
misconceptions: Assuming the major axis is always along the x-axis regardless of which denominator is larger
Putting the foci on the minor axis instead of the major axis

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of an ellipse as 2b^2/a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of an ellipse and show its length is 2b^2/a.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of an ellipse is a line segment perpendicular to the major axis through any of the foci and whose end points lie on the ellipse
nodes.explanation: The latus rectum of an ellipse is the chord through a focus, perpendicular to the major axis, with ends on the ellipse. Put x = c = ae into x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1; the half-length l satisfies l^2 = b^2(1 - e^2) = b^4/a^2, so l = b^2/a. By symmetry the full latus rectum is 2b^2/a. So for x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1 (a = 5, b = 3) the latus rectum is 2(9)/5 = 18/5. This completes the standard read-off list for an ellipse alongside foci, vertices and eccentricity.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the major axis
Substitute x = ae into the standard equation to get the half-length b^2/a
Double it for the full length 2b^2/a
misconceptions: Quoting the latus rectum as b^2/a (the half-length) instead of 2b^2/a
Using 2a^2/b by swapping the roles of a and b

nodes.title: Define a hyperbola by the constant difference of focal distances
nodes.brief: State that a hyperbola is the locus of points whose distances from two foci differ by a constant, and name its centre, transverse and conjugate axes and vertices.
nodes.quote: A hyperbola is the set of all points in a plane, the difference of whose distances from two fixed points in the plane is a constant.
nodes.explanation: A hyperbola is the set of points the difference of whose distances from two fixed points (the foci) is a constant - the distance to the farther focus minus the distance to the nearer one. The midpoint of the segment joining the foci is the centre. The line through the foci is the transverse axis and the perpendicular line through the centre is the conjugate axis; the points where the curve meets the transverse axis are the vertices. This constant-difference condition PF1 - PF2 = 2a is the geometric basis for the hyperbola's equation, and it is the natural counterpart of the ellipse's constant sum.
keyMoves: State the locus condition PF1 - PF2 = constant (= 2a)
Read 'difference' as farther distance minus nearer distance
Identify centre, transverse axis, conjugate axis and the vertices
misconceptions: Using the sum of focal distances (that defines an ellipse, not a hyperbola)
Confusing the transverse axis (through the foci) with the conjugate axis

nodes.title: Relate the axes and focal distance of a hyperbola (b^2 = c^2 - a^2)
nodes.brief: Use 2c (distance between foci), 2a (transverse axis) and the defining quantity b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2), with 2b the conjugate axis.
nodes.quote: Also 2b is the length of the conjugate axis
nodes.explanation: For a hyperbola denote the distance between the foci by 2c and the distance between the vertices (the transverse axis) by 2a, and define b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2), so b^2 = c^2 - a^2, i.e. c^2 = a^2 + b^2. Then 2b is the length of the conjugate axis. Note the contrast with the ellipse: here c > a (so c^2 = a^2 + b^2), whereas for an ellipse a > c (a^2 = b^2 + c^2). This relation lets you pass between the foci, vertices and the conjugate axis when reading or building a hyperbola.
keyMoves: Set distance between foci = 2c, transverse axis = 2a
Define b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2), i.e. c^2 = a^2 + b^2, with 2b the conjugate axis
Note c > a, so c^2 = a^2 + b^2 (the opposite sign-pattern to the ellipse)
misconceptions: Using a^2 = b^2 + c^2 (the ellipse relation) instead of c^2 = a^2 + b^2
Taking the transverse axis to be the conjugate axis

nodes.title: Compute the eccentricity of a hyperbola as e = c/a (>= 1)
nodes.brief: Define the eccentricity e = c/a of a hyperbola and note it is never less than one, with the foci at distance ae from the centre.
nodes.quote: is called the *eccentricity of the hyperbola*. Since
nodes.explanation: Just as for an ellipse, the eccentricity of a hyperbola is e = c/a, the ratio of the centre-to-focus distance to the centre-to-vertex distance. But since for a hyperbola c >= a, the eccentricity is never less than one (e >= 1), in contrast with the ellipse's e < 1. In terms of e the foci lie at distance ae from the centre. For example, x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1 has a = 3, b = 4, c = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5, so e = 5/3 > 1. The eccentricity again captures the curve's shape in one number.
keyMoves: Compute c from c^2 = a^2 + b^2, then form e = c/a
Note e >= 1 for a hyperbola (versus e < 1 for an ellipse)
Place the foci at distance ae from the centre
misconceptions: Expecting e < 1 for a hyperbola
Computing c from a^2 = b^2 + c^2 instead of c^2 = a^2 + b^2

nodes.title: Use the standard equation x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 of a hyperbola
nodes.brief: Apply the standard equation of a hyperbola, read the transverse axis from the positive term, and extract foci, vertices and lengths.
nodes.quote: These two equations are known as the standard equations of hyperbolas.
nodes.explanation: With centre at the origin and foci on the x-axis, the constant-difference condition gives x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1; with foci on the y-axis it is y^2/a^2 - x^2/b^2 = 1. The transverse axis lies along the variable of the positive term, and the foci are always on the transverse axis. Comparing a given equation with the standard form gives a and b, then c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) gives the foci, the vertices (+-a, 0) or (0, +-a), and the transverse-axis length 2a. (A hyperbola with a = b is called equilateral.) To build a hyperbola from conditions, choose the form by where the foci lie and solve for a^2 and b^2.
keyMoves: Identify the transverse axis from the positive term (foci lie on it)
Read a and b from the standard form and compute c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2)
Report foci, vertices and transverse-axis length, or solve for a^2, b^2 from given data
misconceptions: Taking the transverse axis from the larger denominator rather than the positive term
Using c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2) (the ellipse formula) for a hyperbola

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of a hyperbola as 2b^2/a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of a hyperbola and use its length 2b^2/a.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of hyperbola is a line segment perpendicular to the transverse axis through any of the foci and whose end points lie on the hyperbola.
nodes.explanation: The latus rectum of a hyperbola is the chord through a focus, perpendicular to the transverse axis, with ends on the curve. As in the ellipse, its length works out to 2b^2/a. So for x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1 (a = 3, b = 4) the latus rectum is 2(16)/3 = 32/3. This completes the standard read-off list for a hyperbola - foci, vertices, eccentricity and latus rectum - all obtained once the equation is matched to its standard form.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the transverse axis
Apply the length formula 2b^2/a
Combine with the standard form to report all of foci, vertices, eccentricity, latus rectum
misconceptions: Using 2a^2/b by swapping a and b
Measuring the latus rectum along the transverse axis instead of perpendicular to it

nodes.title: Derive standard conic equations from their geometric definitions
nodes.brief: Turn the focus/directrix or distance-sum/difference definition of any conic into its standard equation and read off its key features.
nodes.quote: In the following sections, we shall obtain the equations of each of these conic sections in standard form by defining them based on geometric properties.
nodes.explanation: The whole chapter follows one method: define each conic as a locus by a geometric property, then apply the distance formula to that property to get a standard equation, and finally read its key features off the equation. A circle (equidistant from a centre) gives (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2; a parabola (equidistant from focus and directrix) gives y^2 = 4ax with latus rectum 4a; an ellipse (constant sum of focal distances) gives x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 with a^2 = b^2 + c^2, e = c/a < 1, latus rectum 2b^2/a; a hyperbola (constant difference) gives x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 with c^2 = a^2 + b^2, e = c/a >= 1, latus rectum 2b^2/a. Given any conic - as a definition, a set of conditions or an equation - you can produce its standard equation and extract its centre/vertex, foci, axes, eccentricity and latus rectum.
keyMoves: Start from the conic's locus definition and apply the distance formula
Simplify to the standard equation and identify a, b, c (and the centre)
Read off vertices, foci, axes, eccentricity and latus rectum, or build the equation from given conditions
misconceptions: Treating the four conics as needing four unrelated methods rather than one locus-to-equation procedure
Mixing up the ellipse relation a^2 = b^2 + c^2 with the hyperbola relation c^2 = a^2 + b^2
